facts.fact: Anthropic prompt caching writes cost 1.25x base input price for 5-minute cache and 2x for 60-minute cache, while cache reads cost only 0.1x base price
Cache hits require 100% byte-for-byte identical prompt segments; a single trailing space, punctuation change, or timestamp difference invalidates the entire cached prefix
A stable prefix strategy achieved 85.2% cache hit rate, reusing an average of 46,059 tokens per request, while perturbed prompts had 0% cache hit rate
Cache reads cost $0.30/MTok versus $3.00/MTok for fresh processing, representing 90% cost savings on cache hits
ProjectDiscovery cut LLM costs by 59% with prompt caching, reaching 66% savings post-optimization and 70% for the last 10 days
Prompt caching reduces latency by up to 80% and input token costs by up to 90% for large repetitive prompts
Removing tokens from the middle of a conversation invalidates the KV cache entirely, causing all tokens after the edit point to be reprocessed from scratch
LLMs exhibit 30%+ accuracy drop on multi-document QA when the answer document moves from position 1 to position 10 in a 20-document context (lost-in-the-middle effect)
PoisonedRAG achieved 90% attack success rate by injecting just 5 malicious texts per target question into a knowledge database with millions of documents
Retrieval poisoning attacks in RAG systems successfully conveyed malicious injected sequences to LLMs in 86.67% of cases after the complex RAG workflow
Google DeepMind's CaMel framework (April 2025) uses dual-LLM pattern: privileged LLM holds tools but never reads untrusted content; quarantined LLM reads untrusted content but cannot take action
OWASP Gen AI Security Project lists prompt injection as the #1 security risk for LLM applications as of 2025

Independent benchmarks show models claiming 200K token context become unreliable around 130K tokens; 1M-token models maintain high-quality recall to approximately 600K-700K tokens before accuracy drops
As of March 13, 2026, Anthropic eliminated the long-context surcharge entirely; a 900K-token request is billed at the same $5.00/MTok input rate as a 9K-token request
A well-designed RAG pipeline retrieves 5–20 relevant chunks totaling 2,000–10,000 tokens, representing 50–200x reduction in input tokens per query compared to full-context approaches
Context placed in the middle of a prompt tends to be overlooked by LLMs due to primacy and recency bias; this is called the 'lost in the middle' phenomenon
Five carefully crafted documents can manipulate AI responses 90% of the time through Retrieval-Augmented Generation poisoning
Cached content hierarchy in Anthropic's caching: tools → system → messages; changes at any level invalidate that level and all subsequent levels
Thinking blocks cannot be cached directly with cache_control, but can be cached when they appear in previous assistant turns alongside other content
At 1M tokens, a single request to Claude Opus 4.6 costs $9.00 in input tokens alone (at $9/MTok base rate) before any output costs

examples.metric: 86.67% of injected sequences successfully conveyed to LLMs after complex RAG workflow

examples.scenario: Chat application with stable system prompts, consistent document retrieval, and repetitive user questions
examples.metric: 70%+ of input tokens cached through prefix caching; 30% handled by semantic caching; combined savings exceeding 80%

analogies.analogy: Like a memoized function result becoming invalid if any input parameter changes by even one bit; the entire downstream computation must run again from scratch
Like reading a long report where you skip the middle pages; you remember the first few points clearly and the last few points vividly, but miss critical details buried in the middle
Like a memoization cache in programming: high hit rate means repeated computations are avoided; low hit rate means you're recalculating expensive operations
Like contaminating a source of drinking water; the poison is mixed into the retrieval results and reaches all downstream systems, affecting every decision made with that context
Like upfront investment with high interest: the 1.25x write cost is expensive upfront, but the 0.1x read cost recovers that investment quickly; breakeven happens after 1-2 reads
Like a firewall: the quarantined LLM reads untrusted content but cannot affect systems; the privileged LLM takes actions but never directly reads untrusted input

key_numbers.context: Anthropic 5-minute cache writes cost 1.25x base input price; 60-minute cache writes cost 2x
Cached reads cost only 10% of base input price, representing 90% savings on repeated context
Real-world deployment with stable prompt structure achieved 85.2% cache hit rate reusing 46,059 tokens/request
ProjectDiscovery achieved 59% initial savings, 66% post-optimization, 70% over last 10 days with prompt caching
Accuracy drops 30%+ when relevant document moves from position 1 to position 10 in 20-document context
Five malicious documents per target question achieved 90% attack success rate in knowledge bases with millions of documents
Injected sequences successfully reached LLMs in 86.67% of cases after complex RAG workflow
65% median TTFT improvement for stable prefixes compared to perturbed prompts
Prompt caching reduces latency by up to 80% for large repetitive prompts
Prompt caching reduces input token costs by up to 90% for large repetitive prompts
Well-designed RAG pipelines retrieve 5-20 chunks (2,000-10,000 tokens) vs full context, reducing input tokens 50-200x
At 1M input tokens, Claude Opus 4.6 costs $9.00 in input tokens alone (before output)
Standard cache entries have minimum lifetime of 5 minutes before deletion
Extended cache entries have minimum lifetime of 60 minutes before deletion
Models claiming 1M tokens maintain high-quality recall to approximately 600K-700K before accuracy drops noticeably
Five carefully crafted documents can manipulate AI responses 90% of the time through RAG poisoning
OWASP Gen AI Security Project ranks prompt injection as #1 security risk for LLM applications (2025)
Models claiming 200K token context become unreliable around 130K tokens in real benchmarks
Correcting positional bias via calibration improved middle-positioned information accuracy by 15 percentage points
Anthropic allows up to 4 explicit cache breakpoints per request

common_misconceptions.misconception: Caching is only worth it if you have 100% identical requests repeatedly
common_misconceptions.truth: Prefix caching works as long as the static portion is identical; variable parts (user queries, timestamps) go after the cache breakpoint. A 70% cached prefix still saves 70% of those tokens on every request

common_misconceptions.misconception: Prompt caching always pays off immediately on the first request
common_misconceptions.truth: Cache writes cost 1.25x input price (5-min) or 2x (1-hr), so breakeven requires 1-2 cache reads. Single-use prompts will never reach breakeven

common_misconceptions.misconception: A 1M token context window means you can safely use all 1M tokens
common_misconceptions.truth: Real-world testing shows models claiming 1M tokens become unreliable around 130K; effective reliable context is 600K-700K. Advertised limits are not practical limits

common_misconceptions.misconception: Placing context in the middle of your prompt is neutral; position doesn't matter
common_misconceptions.truth: LLMs exhibit 30%+ accuracy drops when relevant information moves from the beginning to the middle of the context due to lost-in-the-middle effect. Beginning and end of prompt are strongly preferred

common_misconceptions.misconception: Simple truncation of old conversation history is a safe way to manage context
common_misconceptions.truth: Naive truncation removes important information, reducing accuracy and introducing hallucinations. Intelligent truncation should preserve must-have content (system prompt, current message) and only trim optional context

common_misconceptions.misconception: RAG systems are safer than direct prompts because they retrieve only 'trusted' documents
common_misconceptions.truth: RAG poisoning is highly effective: five crafted documents can manipulate responses 90% of the time; 86.67% of injected sequences reach the LLM after RAG workflow. Retrieval does not equal safety

common_misconceptions.misconception: Clearing untrusted content from the prompt is sufficient defense against prompt injection
common_misconceptions.truth: Prompt injection is a system-level vulnerability; defense requires architecture (dual-LLM patterns, trust boundaries, strict output validation), not just prompt hygiene

common_misconceptions.misconception: If you add a space or change a timestamp, the cache is still valid
common_misconceptions.truth: Cache requires 100% byte-for-byte identical text. A single trailing space, punctuation change, or timestamp reformatting invalidates the entire cached prefix

common_misconceptions.misconception: Removing a token from the middle of a conversation invalidates only that token's cache
common_misconceptions.truth: Removing or editing tokens from the middle of a prompt invalidates the entire KV cache from that point forward; all downstream tokens must be reprocessed from scratch due to causal attention

common_misconceptions.misconception: Context cost is purely about input token count × model price
common_misconceptions.truth: Total cost includes cache write premium (1.25-2x), cache read discount (0.1x), and output tokens (which cost 3-10x more than input). A naive calculation misses 80%+ of the cost structure

common_misconceptions.misconception: A stable prompt structure is the only lever for cache hit rates
common_misconceptions.truth: Cache hit rate depends on: prefix stability (100% match required), request frequency (enough volume to reuse), and time within cache lifetime. Low request volume means cache never hits despite stable prefixes

common_misconceptions.misconception: Dynamic context injection is about randomly selecting the most relevant documents
common_misconceptions.truth: Advanced dynamic context assembly adapts k (number of chunks retrieved) to query complexity using a classifier, avoids lost-in-the-middle placement, and uses re-ranking (cross-encoder or LLM-based) to order results
